title: Today's AI Brief: AI Agents Plunged the Tech World Into Chaos. Here’s Exactly How That Happened
summary: AI agents cause chaos in tech, a London meetup for AI addicts, and the implications of AI platform shifts.

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI brief. I am looking at a story from Wired that caught my eye - AI agents have plunged the tech world into chaos. Let's dive into what that means and how it happened. It's a tale of rapid growth, unexpected consequences, and a dash of addiction.

It all started with a meetup in London called Claude Code Anonymous. The attendees, self-described 'Claudeholics,' gathered to network and share their experiences with AI agents. The event's organizer, Peter Steinberger, candidly shared his struggles with AI addiction. This anecdote highlights the growing impact of AI on our lives. It's intriguing to see how AI can bring people together, even if it's to discuss their struggles with it.

Steinberger's story is interesting because it shows how AI can be both helpful and overwhelming. His experience with Claude, an AI agent, is a great example of the blurred lines between human and machine interactions. My read is that this is just the beginning of a larger conversation about AI safety and trust. As AI agents become more prevalent, we need to consider the potential risks and consequences of our increasing reliance on them.

AI Agents Plunged the Tech World Into Chaos. Here’s Exactly How That Happened is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: “Hi, my name is Peter, and I’m a Claudeholic.” It was August 2025 and Peter Steinberger was addressing a meetup in London called Claude Code Anonymous. Steinberger and some fellow addicts had arranged the event to network with people like themselves—techies swept up by coding tools such as Anthropic ’s paradigm-busting Claude Code. “I dedicate pretty much a... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
